Specification guidelines for pesticides formulated and/or prepared as devices Use sharp scissors, or equivalent, to minimize damage to the fibres and fabric and thus avoid any consequential bias in the results of certain tests. Roll up the strips or squares and place them in labelled, new, clean aluminium foil prior to analysis. Samples should be kept cool, avoiding heat sources (including direct sunlight) or freezing, and analysed/tested with minimum delay. Representative portions (sub-samples) for testing should be taken as described in each test method. For the purposes of chemical analysis, the analytical method and the number and size of test portions analysed should be designed to provide results with relative standard deviation (RSD) ≤ % or as applicable in certain justifiable cases. Test portion and replication requirements for physical test methods are defined in the methods or Notes referenced. Note If the netting is manufactured under different denier, the active ingredient (and synergist) content has to be specified for each type of denier. If the active ingredient content is also specified as mg/m2 of netting, this should be calculated from values for active ingredient in /kg and mass of net/m2. Mass of net/m2 should be determined according to ISO 3801 / EN 12127. In cases of dispute, /kg values shall be used. Note This clause or sub-clause is required only if appropriate to the product specified. Isomer ratio is specified only where the active ingredient is defined as particular isomer ratio. synergist is specified only where required. An impurity is specified only where it is relevant, as defined in the glossary of terms (Appendix ). Note The method of analysis must be peer-validated, as minimum. If it is not published, full details of the method and the peer-validation data must be provided. Note 10 In the absence of simple or standard method to determine the size of holes, which may have complex shapes, in highly flexible fabrics, mesh size is determined by counting the number of holes in square of the fabric. Counting may be done directly on the fabric or indirectly by taking picture/photocopy of the fabric. Indirect methods may ease counting and provide permanent record. The number of holes per measured area is converted in holes/cm2. Before counting, the fabric should be conditioned according to ISO 139 ( , 20ºC, 65% relative humidity). Use template to define the square of netting, taking care not to stretch or distort the fabric. The template should be - mm thick rigid sheet, in/on which an accurately calibrated (±% in each dimension) square (.. in or cm) has been cut/marked. Appropriate template size depends on each LN. Note the suitable template size. If template is not available and ruler must be used, great care is required to ensure that the area counted is square. Where practicable, one edge of the square to be counted should be aligned with row of complete holes in the fabric. Incomplete holes ≥ ½ are counted as complete holes, whereas those < ½ are not counted. Count replicate squares selected according to Note , calculate the average and note the lowest value. Another suitable method is the use of stereomicroscope with an image analyser software, where the number of holes in defined area is counted. In case of discrepancy between the netting mesh size using stereomicroscopic method and direct or indirect counting method, the stereomicroscopic method shall be the referee method. Note 11 Method of preparation, marking and measuring: ISO 3759. Method of washing: ISO 6330. Method of calculation: ISO 5077. Size of test portions: 500 mm 500 mm; mark off 350 mm 350 mm within each test portion. Test total of replicate portions, washed in each of separate loads. Type of washing machine: ISO type (front loading). Washing programme: 30ºC Mild programme. Fill the washer with fabrics and ballast Type III (polyester ballast) up to kg (according to the ISO 6330 standard). Drying: flat drying. …./cont. 207

Manual on development and use of FAO and WHO specifications for chemical pesticides Note 12 Test method: ISO 13938 part with conditioning of the fabric as specified in the ISO standard. The declared bursting strength, and testing for compliance with it, should be based on tests of . cm2 areas of fabric. Proposed specifications based on tests of 50 cm2 area must be supported by data showing the suitability of the proposed value and its relationship to minimum of 250 kPa (which is based on . cm2 area). Five replicate tests should be conducted on samples taken at approximately equal distances on diagonal across the netting, taking no sample within 10 cm of border or seam. In made up rectangular nets, the “diagonal” may correspond to Figure . The average of the measurements is calculated. The method to test seam bursting strength is identical to that used to test the fabric, except that replicate tests should be made, with the seam centred on the test head. Up to seams may be tested but, if there are < seams, replicate measurements should be made on or more seams, to provide total of measurements. Note 13 Flammability test according to EN 1102, using the surface ignition method (position the burner perpendicular to the surface of the specimen). The following observations shall be reported: the afterflame time, the afterglow time, the maximum burnt or damage width and length, whether or not flame reaches vertical edge of the specimen, whether or not hole is burnt or melted in the sample, whether or not any flaming debris falls below the bottom edge of the sample and ignition of the filter paper. Definitions according to ISO 4880:1997 (not included in EN 1102:2016 and EN ISO 6941: 1995): Ignition: initiation of combustion. Combustion: exothermic reaction of combustible substance with an oxidizer, accompanied by flames and/or glowing and/or emission of smoke. Procedure for measuring burnt or damage width and length dimensions of each sample: Remove the sample from the sample holder and place it on flat horizontal surface. Place rule on top of the test sample along the line of maximum damage and parallel with the length side of the test specimen. Measure the maximum length in millimetres from the lowest point of burnt or damage to the end of the hole. To measure the burnt or damage width, proceed in the same way but with the ruler parallel to the width side of the test sample. Proceed in the same way for the other samples. In the case of combination LN, an appropriate sampling figure should be provided. .22 MATRIX RELEASE Introduction Matrix release (MR) in this guideline is mainly for public health protection uses but could potentially also be used in agricultural applications in the future. This formulation type can be classified into the following two basic forms. ) One or more active ingredient() are either incorporated into polymer, or coated onto the surface of polymer. 209

Manual on development and use of FAO and WHO specifications for chemical pesticides ) In some cases, both methods (coating and incorporation) are applied for the preparation of finished MR formulation (combination type). Combination matrix formulation is composed of different types of formulations such as coated and incorporated, which are produced by different preparation methods. For combination type MR it is advisable to split the specification into one specification for each polymer preparation type and another for the finished product. The specifications and footnotes should be modified accordingly to cover the clauses properly. For such product, two specifications which refer to each preparation method are then combined into specification for finished product. matrix release consists of one or more active ingredients, polymer and formulants if appropriate. Its size and weight is defined by manufacturing and/or use requirements. It is intended for direct application into body of water. Generally, for public health protection, this formulation shall realize long lasting pest efficacy by controlled release of active ingredient() after application into the habitat of harmful pests, for example, water source, pond, water jar or well. Therefore, selection of active ingredient(), content of active ingredient(), product design (shape or size) and retention/release rate of active ingredient() are important parameters for defining the quality of this formulation type. These parameters can be optimized by the manufacturing process and/or customer Note for preparation of draft specifications. Do not omit clauses or insert additional needs. clauses, nor insert limits that are more lax than those than given in the guidelines, without referring to Section . For combination MR, specifications must be separated into one specification for each material and one for the finished product. Recommended wrapping in aluminium foil for individual MR storage (MT 46.) .23 LONG-LASTING STORAGE BAGS Introduction “long-lasting storage bag” (LB) is woven polymer fibre bag with pesticide active ingredient incorporated into the fibre or coated on the surface of the fibre. The storage bag is intended for postharvest storage of commodities including cereal grains, pulses and seeds and provides control of pests before they can infest the stored commodities. The pesticide is released to the surface of the material in sustained manner so that the commodities stored in the bags are continuously protected against pest infestation over certain time period (at least years, over multiple seasons). To evaluate the migration of the active ingredient the proposed methodology lends itself from the CIPAC method MT195 (Wash resistance index of LN’). Whilst it is understood that the bags are unlikely to be washed, it provides validated method for release and migration properties of the active ingredient to the surface of the polymer material. Accelerated storage clauses were divided into two sub sections as sampling and sub sampling for chemical and physical tests require different schemes (.23.. and .23..). In case the fibre material is polyolefin (high-density polyethylene [HDPE], polypropylene [PP]), an anti-slip weave in the weaving pattern is recommended. Storage bags made of .. polypropylene tend to slip when stacked and therefore present hazard. The anti-slip weave increases the frictional force between stacked bags and reduces the hazard of falling bags. The presence of an anti-slip weave may be checked by visual inspection counting ends versus picks and comparison with known anti-slip weaving patterns. The anti-slip weave should be noted on the label of the sFtooordag ceo bnatga. material The polymer material used to produce the yarn must be virgin (non-recycled) polymer and comply with international regulations concerning food contact material. 214

Note Method() of analysis must be CIPAC, AOAC or equivalent. Where methods have not yet been published, full details and appropriate method validation data must be submitted to FAO/WHO by the proposer. The sampling scheme should consider the within-product variation. For active ingredient content, homogenization of sample before taking portions before and after storage and keeping the sample in capped bottle are recommended. Note recommended sampling scheme is presented in Figure below. Samples must be sufficiently large to conduct all tests required and representative of the fabric. Except where seams are to be tested, do not test material within 10 cm of seams or selvedge. Use sharp scissors, or equivalent, to minimize damage to the fibres and fabric and thus avoid any consequential bias in the results of certain tests. Roll up the strips or squares and place them in labelled, new, clean aluminium foil prior to analysis. Samples should be kept cool, avoiding heat sources (including direct sunlight) or freezing, and analysed/tested with minimum delay. Representative portions (sub-samples) for testing should be taken as described in each test method. Note This clause or sub-clause is required only if appropriate to the product specified. Isomer ratio is specified only where the active ingredient is defined as particular isomer ratio. Note Methods for determination of relevant impurities must be peer validated, as minimum. If it is not published, full details of the method and the peer-validation data must be provided. Note Unless other temperatures and times are specified. Refer to Section .. for alternative storage conditions. Note 10 Samples of the formulation taken before and after the accelerated storage stability test may be analysed concurrently after the test in order to reduce the analytical error. The manufacturer shall demonstrate the sub-sampling design provides homogenized analytical portions for before and after storage stability test. Note 11 The CIPAC method MT 46. is used where the entire bag is stored at 54 ± ° for weeks in order to test the physical properties and appropriate samples are taken after the storage from the bag. Note 12 UV radiation and weathering test to be conducted as per procedure given in ISO 4892- :2013, Table , Method , Cycle No.. 218

APPENDIX SUPPLY AND CERTIFICATION OF REFERENCE SUBSTANCES OF PESTICIDE ACTIVE INGREDIENTS INTRODUCTION Reference substances of known content are often essential for the quantitative determination of pesticide active ingredients in different types of sample and at various concentration levels. They are available from various sources. It is important that the active ingredient content is declared and certified (Note ). . SUPPLY OF REFERENCE SUBSTANCES • here are two sources for the supply of certified reference substances: • the manufacturer of the active ingredient in question; standards institutions or companies selling reference substances. . Supply by the manufacturer The manufacturer of pesticide technical grade active ingredient, or its formulations, is obliged to supply certified reference substance in the f1o.1ll.o1w ingT craasdees : elations between customer and manufacturer The customer, or an independent laboratory, might want to check the active ingredient content of material delivered by the manufacturer. This is to be done on the basis of the specification and using the analytical method stated therein. The manufacturer' certified reference substances should be made available to the customer so as to avoid any discrepancy in the analytical results, which could otherwise be derived from differences in the purity of the reference .. Rsueblasttiaonncse bse utsweede bny thaen utwfaoc tpuarretri easn. registration authorities Each company (not only the inventor company) applying for the registration of pesticide (new .. or formulation) must supply its own certified reference substance, if required by the registration authorities. . Supply by reference substances producers or suppliers There are various standards institutions and commercial companies which offer reference substances. They should normally be approached if reference substances are required in situations different from those mentioned above. On request manufacturers may, at their discretion, also supply reference substances in such cases. 227

Manual on development and use of FAO and WHO specifications for chemical pesticides . CERTIFICATION certificate of identity and determined content, together with the corresponding measurement uncertainty and reference to the method() of analysis used, must always accompany each sample of certified reference substance. Descriptions such as “greater than ..%” are unacceptable. The recommended storage conditions and an expiry date shall be stated. Note : For more detailed information, see CIPAC Handbook , pages 186-196. 228

APPENDIX GLOSSARY OF TERMS Term Definition Active ingredient() Active ingredient means the part of the product that provides the pesticidal action. Additive An ingredient, other than the MPCA, intentionally added to formulation Agglomerate Particles bound firmly together. Aggregate Particles adhering loosely together. ADI Acceptable daily intake. AgroCare global association representing the independent post-patent crop protection industry. AOAC AOAC International, formerly the Association of Official Analytical Chemists. Apparent density see Density. ARfD Acute reference dose. ASTM American Society for Testing and Materials. Attrition The wearing away of the surface of solid by friction or impact, particularly by particle-to-particle interaction. See also Friability. Batch defined quantity of material produced in single series of operations. Bt Bacillus thuringiensis Bulk density see Density. CA Chemical Abstracts®. Carrier solid formulant added to technical grade active ingredient as an absorbent or diluent. CAS® No. Chemical Abstracts Service® registry number. CFU Colony forming units CIPAC Collaborative International Pesticides Analytical Council. Co-formulant Co-formulant means non-active ingredient component of formulated product. CropLife International Formerly known as GCPF and also GIFAP. Closed meeting meeting of the JMPS dealing with confidential information, where participation is confined exclusively to experts appointed by FAO/WHO. Proposers and/or others may be invited by FAO/WHO for consideration of specific issues. Compatibility The absence of adverse or unwanted reactions/interactions (physical, chemical or biological) when chemicals or formulations are mixed together. Contaminant (biological) For the purposes of this manual, any unexpected biological entity or parts thereof (other than components which may be considered as chemical contaminants), occurring by any means in technical or formulated pesticide. See also Impurity. 229

Manual on development and use of FAO and WHO specifications for chemical pesticides Term Definition Contaminant (chemical) For the purposes of this manual, an unexpected substance or material, or mixture, occurring by any means in technical or formulated pesticide. See also Impurity. Cream An opaque layer accumulating at the top or the bottom of an emulsion. Density Mass per unit volume of substance at stated temperature. The units of volume and mass must be stated, .. grams per millilitre at 20 ± 2ºC. Bulk density of powders and granules refers to their apparent density, including air, etc., incorporated into the bulk. Bulk density values are affected by settling (.. by tapping), compaction or pressure. Device For the purposes of this manual, any physical or mechanical entity which is loaded with quantity of pesticide, ready for immediate use without dilution, mixing, etc. Dispersibility The ease with which an insoluble solid or liquid material may be dispersed uniformly in liquid. Dust fine solid material, potentially airborne, with particle size less than 50 µm. ECCA European Crop Care Association Ecotoxicological profile summary of data on ecotoxicological endpoints that may have consequences for aquatic and terrestrial organisms, due to possible exposure dependent on the intended uses, for particular pesticide. EDQM European Directorate for the Quality of Medicines and HealthCare. ELINCS No. European List of Notified Chemical Substances number (for new chemicals). EINECS No. European Inventory of Existing Commercial Chemical Substances number (for existing chemicals). Equivalence (equivalent) The FAO/WHO International Code of Conduct on Pesticide Management defines equivalence broadly as: “equivalence means the determination of the similarity of the impurity and toxicological profile, as well as of the physical and chemical properties, presented by supposedly similar technical material originating from different manufacturers, in order to assess whether they present similar levels of risk.” In practice, determination of equivalence by the JMPS involves comparative assessment of the impurity and toxicological profiles, the manufacturing specification as well as data for the physical and chemical properties of technical grade active ingredients (TC/TK) produced by different manufacturers or by different manufacturing routes or on different manufacturing sites. The comparison is made with the reference profile in each case. If the materials can share common specification, and if the degree of similarity is such that the material() produced by the additional manufacturer(), or the new manufacturing route() or sites, present() risks that are considered to be no greater than the TC/TK on which the reference profiles are based, the additional/new material() can be considered equivalent to the original TC/TK. Formulations of particular pesticide are regarded as equivalent if they are prepared from equivalent TCs/TKs and conform to the same specification but this does not imply that they necessarily provide equal efficacy or present identical risks in particular application. 230

Appendix – Glossary of terms Term Definition Endpoint Measurable physico-chemical, ecological or toxicological characteristic or parameter of the test system (usually an organism) that is chosen as the most relevant assessment criterion (.. temperature of decomposition, death in an acute test or tumour incidence in chronic study). Evaluator An expert attending the JMPS, assigned by FAO/WHO to perform the evaluation of data provided in support of proposed FAO/WHO specification, or of proposed extension to an existing specification, following the procedural principles laid down in the current edition of this manual. FAO Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations. FAO EPS FAO Experts on Pesticide Specifications. FAO/WHO specifications International standards of quality for pesticides evaluated and published by FAO/WHO. Filler An inert solid formulant used as diluent. Fines see Undersize particles. Flammable Readily ignitable. Flammable liquid liquid having flash point of not less than 21° and not more than 55°, as determined by closed cup method. See also Highly flammable liquid. Flash point The lowest temperature at which material forms flammable vapour/air mixture under standard conditions. Flocculation Aggregation of particles suspended in liquid. Flowability Ability of materials to flow freely under stated conditions. Formulant Any substance, other than technical grade active ingredient, intentionally incorporated in formulation. Formulation Formulation means the combination of various ingredients designed to render the product useful and effective for the purpose claimed and for the envisaged mode of application. Friability The tendency of solid, such as granule or tablet, to disintegrate by crumbling. See also Attrition. GHS Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labelling of Chemicals. GLP Good Laboratory Practice (OECD Principles). GMO Genetically modified organisms (GMOs) are organisms (.. plants, animals or microorganisms) in which the genetic material (DNA) has been altered by genetic engineering. Hazard Inherent property of an agent or situation having the potential to cause adverse effects when an organism, system, or (sub)population is exposed to that agent. See also Risk. HDPE High-density polyethylene. Highly flammable liquid liquid having flash point of less than 21° as determined by closed cup method. See also Flammable liquid. 231

Manual on development and use of FAO and WHO specifications for chemical pesticides Term Definition Impurity (biological) biological entity or parts thereof (other than components which may be considered as chemical contaminants) arising from manufacture of an active ingredient derived from biological source. For the purposes of this manual, the definition does not include impurities derived from formulants or other additives. See also Contaminant and Relevant impurity. Impurity (chemical) by-product arising from manufacture of the active ingredient or derived from the active ingredient during formulation or storage. For the purposes of this manual, the definition does not include impurities derived solely from formulants or other additives, before or during storage. See also Contaminant and Relevant impurity. INCI No. International Nomenclature of Cosmetic Ingredients number. Independent laboratory See peer validation. validation Interested parties Organizations or individuals, such as commercial companies, pesticide registration authorities, non-governmental organizations, and scientists concerned with pesticide specifications. IPCS International Programme on Chemical Safety. ISO International Organization for Standardization, which publishes common names for pesticides which have generally been developed by the British Standards Institution (BSI). -ISO indicates the English form of the name and -ISO indicates the French form. French names are identified as masculine () or feminine () as appropriate. IUPAC International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry. JECFA Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives. JMPR FAO/WHO Joint Meeting on Pesticide Residues. Comprised of the FAO Panel of Experts on Pesticide Residues in Food and the Environment and the WHO Core Assessment Group on Pesticide Residues. JMPS FAO/WHO Joint Meeting on Pesticide Specifications. group of experts appointed by FAO and WHO to deal with pesticide specifications. LD Lethal dose. LOAEL Lowest observed adverse effect level. Lot Part or all of consignment that may comprise part of, all of, one manufacturing batch. Lump macroscopic piece of solid matter without regular shape. Manual The current edition or revision of the manual on the development and use of FAO/WHO specifications for microbial or chemical pesticides. Manufacturer Manufacturer means corporation or other entity in the public or private sector (including an individual) engaged in the business or function (whether directly or through an agent or entity controlled by or under contract with it) of manufacturing pesticide active ingredient 232

Appendix – Glossary of terms Term Definition Manufacturing Minimum purity of the active ingredient in technical grade active specification ingredient together with the identity and maximum concentrations of all impurities (including “unknowns”) produced by manufacturer using single process, derived from analysis of representative production batches. In general, the impurities are those with manufacturing specification limits at or above /kg but lower limits apply to exceptionally hazardous impurities. Where the same active ingredient is produced at different sites by the same manufacturer and manufacturing route, the profile should encompass all sites. Where the manufacturing route differs between sites, or the manufacturers differ, the impurity profiles should be defined separately. Whereas the minimum purity of the active ingredient and on identity and maximum levels of relevant impurities after evaluation are published in the specification, the information on non-relevant impurities is kept confidential. Metabolites Metabolites include products resulting from degradative and biosynthetic reactions taking place within the microorganism or other organisms used to produce the microorganism of interest. Microbial impurity, Microbiological contamination refers to the non-intended or accidental microbial contaminants introduction of microbes such as bacteria, yeast, virus, prions, protozoa or their toxins and by-products into MPCA or MPCP. Microbial Pest Control MPCA and MPCP: for microbial pest control agents and -products and are Agents and Products used to differentiate these kind of pesticides from the synthetic chemical (MPCA and MPCP) ones ("pesticides" in general), botanicals (plant extracts) and semiochemicals (pheromonones). MPCA can be defined as: microorganism (protozoan, fungus, bacterium, virus, or other microscopic self-replicating biotic entity) (revised ISPM Pub. No. . IPPC, 2005) and any associated metabolites, to which the effects of pest control are attributed (OECD, 2008). microorganism active substance may contain viable and/or non-viable microorganisms. It can contain relevant metabolites/toxins produced during cell proliferation (growth), material from the growth medium, provided none of these components have been intentionally altered. MPCP can be defined as: product containing an MPCA that is registered or labelled with instructions for direct use or application for pest control purposes. Minimum data Data required to evaluate proposals for FAO/WHO specifications. Such data requirements are the minimum considered necessary to evaluate all aspects of the specification. MRL Maximum residue limit. MT Method of testing. NOAEL No observed adverse effect level. NOEL No observable effect levels. Non-flammable Not readily ignitable, with flash point above 55° as determined by closed cup method. OECD Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development. 233

Manual on development and use of FAO and WHO specifications for chemical pesticides Term Definition Open meeting meeting jointly organized by JMPS and CIPAC where, in addition to experts invited by FAO/WHO, participation is open to anyone who wishes to attend. Oversize particles Particles of solid material larger than specified size. Peer validation Validation of an analytical method by (peer) laboratory operating (also known as independently from that of the originator of the method. The two Independent laboratory laboratories may belong to the same organisation, as long as the analysts, validation ILV) equipment, etc., are distinct and operate separately and without collusion for the validation. The validation process will follow the peer verification procedure of AOAC International (or similar). Pesticide Pesticide means any substance, or mixture of substances of chemical or biological ingredients intended for repelling, destroying or controlling any pest, or regulating plant growth. In the context of the manual, the term includes any substance, or mixture of substances, or micro-organisms including viruses, intended for repelling, destroying or controlling any pest, including vectors of human or animal disease, nuisance pests, unwanted species of plants or animals causing harm during or otherwise interfering with the production, processing, storage, transport, or marketing of food, agricultural commodities, wood and wood products or animal feeding stuffs, or which may be administered to animals for the control of insects, arachnids or other pests in or on their bodies. The term includes substances intended for use as insect or plant growth regulators; defoliants; desiccants; agents for setting, thinning or preventing the premature fall of fruit; and substances applied to crops either before or after harvest to protect the commodity from deterioration during storage and transport. The term also includes pesticide synergists and safeners, where they are integral to the satisfactory performance of the pesticide. The term “technical pesticide” refers to technical materials and technical concentrates. The term “formulated pesticide” refers to any formulation containing pesticide. pH Potential hydrogen. Phytotoxic Phytotoxicity is the capacity of compound to cause temporary or long- lasting damage to plants. The damage may be general or restricted to certain species or cultivars of plants. Phytotoxic impurities or contaminants in herbicide may extend the range of plants damaged beyond that expected. POP Persistent organic pollutant. Proposer Any manufacturer, group of manufacturers, or interested party, which submits draft specification and data package, to FAO/WHO for evaluation, in support of new specification or for extension of an existing specification. ()SAR Quantitative structure–activity relationship. 234

Appendix – Glossary of terms Term Definition Reference specification The current published specification for pesticide, where this has been developed according to evaluation procedures similar to that given in this manual (.. 1999-on for FAO specifications and 2002-on for WHO specifications). The reference specification is subject to review and may be revised in the light of emergent information, or to incorporate the formulations of subsequent manufacturer. The reference specification is used as the first criterion in the determination of equivalence of technical grade active ingredient and/or formulation of parallel or subsequent manufacturer. Reference profile The purity/impurity, toxicological and ecotoxicological profiles upon which the original specification for technical grade active ingredient is based. The reference profiles are used for the determination of equivalence. reference profile is not amended by the data supporting additional technical grade active ingredients that are subsequently judged to be equivalent but, following review of specifications by the JMPS, new reference profile may supersede an earlier one. Generally, the reference profile of impurities relates to the technical grade active ingredient supported by the most complete toxicological and ecotoxicological profiles. Release date The date from which the supplier guarantees shelf-life of at least years, unless stated otherwise, under actual conditions of storage in the area where the technical grade active ingredient or formulation is to be marketed. Relevant impurity by-product of the manufacture or storage of pesticide which, compared with the active ingredient, is toxicologically significant to health or the environment, is phytotoxic to treated plants, causes taint in food crops, affects the stability of the pesticide, or causes any other adverse effect. Water may be relevant impurity if it can adversely affect the stability of the pesticide or the manufacture of satisfactory formulation. Insoluble material may also be relevant impurity in TC/TK if formulations to be prepared from them would block spray filters/nozzles, or fail the wet sieve test, for example. An impurity may be non-relevant in one pesticide or product and relevant in another, even though it occurs in both, because relevance is determined by impurity hazards relative to those of the active ingredient. Relevant metabolites Metabolites that are of concern for human or animal health and/or the environment. Relevant Compounds added to MPCP in relatively small amounts to effect desired additive/stabilizer property (additive) or enhance the stability of the MPCA (stabilizer). Risk The probability of an adverse effect in an organism, system, or (sub)population caused under specified circumstances by exposure to an agent. RSD Relative standard deviation. Sedimentation The fall of particles in continuous medium (usually liquid for specification purposes). Seeds The term "seeds" as used in this manual with regard to seed treatment encompasses all kind of plant material that can be sown, .. seeds of cereals, "seed" potatoes, stem parts of cassava etc. 235

Manual on development and use of FAO and WHO specifications for chemical pesticides Term Definition Sieving Separation of particles according to their size by the use of sieves. Secondary compound Small organic molecules produced by an organism that are not essential for their growth, development and reproduction. Seed treatment Seed treatment refers to the application of fungicide, insecticide, or combination of both, to seeds so as to disinfect and disinfest them from seed-borne or soil-borne pathogenic organisms and storage insects (cited after ecoport.org). Size distribution The mass or numerical frequency distribution of the particles of solid particulate material. Size range Lower and upper limits in size of particulate material. Specification The Code of Conduct on Pesticide Management broadly defines "Specification means the parameters and criteria defining the physical appearance and physical and chemical properties of technical and formulated pesticides linked with hazard and risk profiles". For more details see Section .., Scope of Specifications. FAO and WHO specifications together with the evaluation reports are published on the respective websites of these two organizations. Subsequent, additional Any pesticide manufacturer other than the proposer of the original or parallel manufacturer specification. Surfactant formulant which reduces the interfacial tension of two boundary surfaces, thereby increasing the emulsifying, spreading, dispersibility and/or wetting properties of liquids or solids. Tank mix Two or more formulations mixed in the spray tank (including non-pesticide formulations .. liquid fertilizers) . Tap density see Density. Taxon In the framework of this guideline on microbial pest control product specifications, the term Taxon is used as proxy for the full taxonomic designation of bacteria, viruses etc. Tolerance Permitted limits of variation for active ingredient content from given value. Known as “certified limits” in some countries. Toxicity Inherent property of an agent to cause an adverse biological effect. Toxicological profile summary of data on toxicological endpoints that may have consequences for human health, due to exposure via various routes, for particular pesticide. Undersize particles Particles of solid material smaller than specified size. UNIDO United Nations Industrial Development Organization. 236

Appendix – Glossary of terms Term Definition Unintentional In contrast to added, chemically characterized active and inert ingredients, ingredients that are present in microbial pesticide product, unintentional ingredients in MPCA or MPCP are not added on purpose. Examples of such extraneous materials may include: allergens, microbial toxins, and other metabolic products; mutant strains; microbial contaminants with particular reference to potentially infective or antagonistic forms; side products from chemical reactions employed in the manufacturing process, fermentation residues from the growth of bacteria or fungi; extraneous host residues from viruses produced in cell cultures, whole animals, or other living forms; residues of contaminants that remain following the purification or extraction process; and impurities in chemicals used in the manufacturing process (Source: US EPA Microbial Pesticide Test Guidelines, OPPTS 885.1300, Discussion of Formation of Unintentional Ingredients ). The formation, presence and possible consequences of unintentiona21l ingredients must be discussed. UV Ultraviolet radiation. Validation Process by which the reliability and relevance of particular approach, method, process, or assessment is established for defined purpose. VCP Vector Control Product Assessment Team (WHO). WHO World Health Organization. WHOPES WHO Pesticide Evaluation Scheme. WHO PQT/VCP WHO Prequalification Unit - Vector Control Product Assessment Team. 21 Accessible under https://nepis.epa.gov/Exe/ZyPDF.cgi/P100IRQS.PDF?Dockey=P100IRQS.PDF 237

APPENDIX CODING OF ACTIVE INGREDIENTS AND SPECIFICATIONS ISO common names are used in specifications and methods of analysis, where possible. Each specification or CIPAC method of analysis is assigned number based on the CIPAC code number for the chemical and its derivatives, forming cross-reference between the FAO/WHO specification and the method of analysis. What The CIPAC code number system is simple approach for an unambiguous numerical coding are CIPAC Code Numbers? of pesticide active ingredients and their variants (for more details see the document ), https://www.cipac.org/index.php/code-numbers (CIPAC, 2021). The compounds are easily identified and retrieved using these codes. Currently, more than 000 pesticides and an extensive lists of variants like esters or salts are covered by the CIPAC coding system. more detailed description of the background of the coding system and updated alphabetical and numerical lists of pesticides and their codes are provided in the download section under https://www.cipac.org/index.php/code-numbers (CIPAC, 021). 238

APPENDIX CATALOGUE OF PESTICIDE FORMULATION TYPES AND INTERNATIONAL CODING SYSTEM – Technical Monograph No. ourtesy ofCropLife International, 2017 Code Term Definition AE Aerosol dispenser container-held formulation which is dispersed generally by propellant as fine droplets or particles upon the actuation of valve. AL Any other liquid liquid not yet designated by specific code, to be applied undiluted. AP Any other powder powder not yet designated by specific code, to be applied undiluted. BR Briquette Solid block designed for controlled release of active ingredient into water. CB Bait concentrate solid or liquid intended for dilution before use as bait. CP Contact powder Rodenticidal or insecticidal formulation in powder form for direct application. Formerly known as tracking powder (TP). CS Capsule suspension stable suspension of capsules in fluid, normally intended for dilution with water before use. DC Dispersible concentrate liquid homogeneous formulation to be applied as solid dispersion after dilution in water. (Note: there are some formulations which have characteristics intermediate between DC and EC). DP Dustable powder free-flowing powder suitable for dusting. DS Powder for dry seed powder for application in the dry state directly to the seed. treatment DT Tablet for direct Formulation in the form of tablets to be applied individually and application directly in the field, and/or bodies of water, without preparation of spraying solution or dispersion EC Emulsifiable concentrate liquid, homogeneous formulation to be applied as an emulsion after dilution in water. EG Emulsifiable granule granular formulation, which may contain water-insoluble formulants, to be applied as an oil-in-water emulsion of the active ingredient() after disintegration in water. EO Emulsion, water in oil fluid, heterogeneous formulation consisting of solution of pesticide in water dispersed as fine globules in continuous organic liquid phase. EP Emulsifiable powder powder formulation, which may contain water-insoluble formulants, to be applied as an oil-in-water emulsion of the active ingredient() after dispersion in water. ES Emulsion for seed stable emulsion for application to the seed either directly or after treatment dilution. 239

APPENDIX DECLARATIONS OF INTERESTS AND CONFIDENTIALITY DECLARATION OF INTERESTS FOR FAO EXPERTS Title of meeting or work to be performed, including description of subject-matter, substance (compounds and organisms), technology or process to be considered: ____________________________________________________________________ Public health considerations and protection of the environment are of primary importance in all Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) technical work. Measures need to be taken to ensure that the best possible assessment of scientific evidence is achieved in an independent atmosphere free of either direct or indirect pressures. Thus, to assure the technical integrity and impartiality of FAO’ work, it is necessary to avoid situations in which financial or other interests might affect the outcome of that work. Each expert is therefore asked to declare any interests that could constitute real, potential or apparent conflict of interest, with respect to his/her involvement in the meeting or work, between () commercial entities and the participant personally, and () commercial entities and the administrative unit with which the participant has an employment relationship. “Commercial entity” refers to any company, association (.., trade association), organization or any other entity of any nature whatsoever, with commercial interests. What is conflict of interest? Conflict of interest means that the expert or his/her partner (“partner” includes spouse or other person with whom /he has similar close personal relationship), or the administrative unit with which the expert has an employment relationship, has financial or other interest that could unduly influence the expert’ position with respect to the subject- matter being considered. An apparent conflict of interest exists when an interest would not necessarily influence the expert but could result in the expert’ objectivity being questioned by others. potential conflict of interest exists with an interest which any reasonable person could be uncertain whether or not should be reported. Different types of financial or other interests, whether personal or with the administrative unit with which the expert has an employment relationship, can be envisaged and the following list, which is not exhaustive, is provided for your guidance. For example, the following types of situations should be declared: . current proprietary interest in substance, technology or process (.. ownership of patent), to be considered in - or otherwise related to the subject matter of - the meeting or work. . current financial interest, .. shares or bonds, in commercial entity with an interest in the subject-matter of the meeting or work (except share holdings through general mutual funds or similar arrangements where the expert has no control over the selection of shares). . An employment, consultancy, directorship, or other position during the past years, whether or not paid, in any commercial entity which has an interest in the subject-matter 245

Manual on development and use of FAO and WHO specifications for chemical pesticides of the meeting/work, or an ongoing negotiation concerning prospective employment or other association with such commercial entity. . Performance of any paid work or research during the past years commissioned by commercial entity with interests in the subject-matter of the meetings or work. . Payment or other support covering period within the past years, or an expectation of support for the future, from commercial entity with an interest in the subject-matter of the meetings or work, even if it does not convey any benefit to the expert personally but which benefits his/her position or administrative unit, .. grant or fellowship or other payment, .. for the purpose of financing post or consultancy. With respect to the above, an interest in competing substance, technology or process, or an interest in or association with, work for or support by commercial entity having direct competitive interest must similarly be disclosed. How to complete this declaration Please complete this declaration and submit it to the FAO Secretariat. Any financial or other interests that could constitute real, potential or apparent conflict of interest should be declared () with respect to yourself or partner, as well as () with respect to the administrative unit with which you have an employment relationship. Only the name of the commercial entity and the nature of the interest is required to be disclosed, no amounts need to be specified (though they may be, if you consider this information to be relevant to assessing the interest). With respect to items and in the list above, the interest should only be declared if it is current. With respect to items , and , any interest during the past years should be declared. If the interest is no longer current, please state the year when it ceased. With respect to item , the interest ceases when financed post or fellowship is no longer occupied, or when support for an activity ceases. Assessment and outcome The information submitted by you will be used to assess whether the declared interests constitute an appreciable real, potential or apparent conflict of interest. Such conflict of interest will, depending on the situation, result in () you being asked not to take part in the portion of the discussion or work affecting that interest, (ii) being asked not to take part in the meeting or work altogether, or (iii) if deemed by FAO to be appropriate to the particular circumstances, and with your agreement, you taking part in the meeting or work and your interest being publicly disclosed. Information disclosed on this Form may be made available to persons outside of FAO only when the objectivity of the meeting or work has been questioned such that the Director- General considers disclosure to be in the best interests of the Organization, and then only after consultation with you. APPENDIX CALCULATION OF WORST-CASE-POSSIBLE CONTRIBUTION BY AN IMPURITY TO THE TOXIC HAZARDS OF THE ACTIVE INGREDIENT (SEE SECTION . .) The calculations shown below are normally utilized by WHO/PCS in advising JMPS. They are presented here for the purposes of transparency. The calculations are based on known concentrations of the active ingredient, and of the 50 impurity in the technical material, and the toxicity (LD , ADI, BMD, NOAEL, LOAEL, etc.) of the impurity and the technical material (TC), (or technical concentrate (TK). The calculations apply the dose-additivity model (SCHER 2011) for the active ingredient and the impurities, as: ) synergistic effects leading to more than additive effect are exceptional at low exposure et al levels; and ) IPCS recommendation is that even for response-additive effects, the model of dose additivity be used as the worst case scenario (Meek ., 2011). They are not used for the assessment of the relevance of impurities that are carcinogenic, mutagenic, sensitizing, toxic to reproduction or development, or irritating to the eye or skin (see Section ., .). APPENDIX CALCULATION OF EXPANDED TOLERANCES FOR THE ACTIVE INGREDIENT CONTENT OF MIXED SOLID FORMULATIONS (SEE SECTION ..) Solid mixtures cannot be blended perfectly but, even if perfection could be achieved, the dispersion of analytical results would remain dependent on the size of test portion analysed. Therefore, the size of test portion to be analysed must be defined in specification for blend of solids. The method of calculating expanded tolerances for such blends is empirical, based on what is routinely achievable in practice with careful mixing of component solids which are well matched in size and density, and by the analysis of appropriately sized test portions. The values for , in the calculations below, are not intended as tolerances for the addition of component to blend, though they inevitably encompass such variations. They provide simple approach to defining limits which are expected to encompass all sources of manufacturing, sampling and analytical variation. alculation () Calculate the upper and lower limits for each active ingredient present in each formulation component of the blend, referring to the tolerances given in Section ... Active ingredient upper limit in its formulation ( ) = /kg declared + tolerance Active ingredient lower limit in its formulation ( ) = /kg declared – tolerance (ii) Calculate the upper and lower limits for each component in the blend, applying the tolerances intended for active ingredient content in Section ... Blend component upper limit ( ) = /kg declared + tolerance Blend component lower limit ( ) = /kg declared – tolerance (iii) Calculate the upper and lower limits for each active ingredient in the blend. Active ingredient upper limit in the blend = ( )/1000 /kg Active ingredient lower limit in the blend = ( )/1000 /kg Example WG formulation , declared to contain active ingredient at 20% (200 /kg), is blended with WG formulation , declared to contain active ingredient at 65% (650 /kg). The declared ratio of formulations : in the blend is 70%:30% and therefore the declared contents of and in the blend are 14% (140 /kg) and 19.% (195 /kg), respectively. 257

Manual on development and use of FAO and WHO specifications for chemical pesticides Active ingredient () From Section .., the tolerance for active ingredient in formulation is ±% (which is equal to ±12 /kg) and therefore its upper and lower limits in are: 200 + 12 = 212 /kg 200 – 12 = 188 /kg (ii) Applying the values in Section .. to the formulation instead of the active ingredient, the tolerance for formulation in the blend is ±25 /kg (±.%) and therefore its upper and lower limits in the blend are: 700 + 25 = 725 /kg 700 – 25 = 675 /kg (iii) The upper and lower limits of active ingredient in the blend are therefore: (212 725)/1000 = 153. /kg (188 675)/1000 = 126. /kg Note. Without expansion, the tolerance for at 140 /kg in the blend would be ±%, implying upper and lower limits of 148. /kg and 131. /kg, respectively. The expanded tolerances do not represent plus and minus exactly the same percentage of the declared value. Active ingredient () From Section .., the tolerance for active ingredient in formulation is ±25 /kg and therefore its upper and lower limits in are: 650 + 25 = 675 /kg 650 – 25 = 625 /kg (ii) Applying the values in Section .. to the formulation instead of the active ingredient, the tolerance for formulation in the blend is ±% (which is equal to ±15 /kg) and therefore its upper and lower limits in the blend are: 300 + 15 = 315 /kg 300 – 15 = 285 /kg (iii) The upper and lower limits of active ingredient in the blend are therefore: (675 315)/1000 = 212. /kg (625 285)/1000 = 178. /kg Note. Without expansion, the tolerance for at 195 /kg in the blend would be ±%, implying upper and lower limits of 206. /kg and 183. /kg, respectively. The expanded tolerances do not represent plus and minus exactly the same percentage of the declared value. 258

APPENDIX ANALYTICAL BRIDGING STUDIES FOR DETERMINATION OF THE CONTENT OF ACTIVE INGREDIENT IN TC, TK AND FORMULATED PRODUCTS Introduction In the context of this manual, the aim of an analytical bridging study is to demonstrate that an in-house analytical method for determination of the content of an active ingredient in technical material and formulated products leads to comparable results as published, collaboratively validated method, .. by CIPAC, AOAC or equivalent. This is done by analyzing the same set of samples of TC, TK and/or formulated products using the collaboratively validated method and the in-house method and by doing statistical evaluation of the results. This Appendix provides some guidance on analytical bridging studies, yet JMPS expects that bridging study may lead to delays in evaluating and concluding on proposer’ data package by JMPS. The data requirements for analytical methods in support of draft specification or extension of published specification are detailed in Sections . and . of this manual. • riefly, CIPAC (or AOAC International or equivalent) analytical methods, if available, have to be used and followed as close as possible in studies supporting FAO/WHO specifications • because they are fully collaboratively tested or peer-validated. CIPAC methods for active ingredient and relevant impurities have to be used also for equivalence. method validation according to, .. EU SANCO/3030 is not sufficient and • does not fulfill the requirements of JMPS. For non-relevant impurities that are present in specific TC, TK and/or formulated product method validation is acceptable. Parameter Validation Active ingredient content CIPAC full method (provisional also accepted, but not draft) Relevant impurities content Peer-validated method .. through CIPAC Non-relevant impurities .. EU SANCO/3030 Physico-chemical properties CIPAC MT method be explained and justified If modifications of CIPAC method were done by proposer, the reason for deviations have to (.. use of methanol instead of acetonitrile in the HPLC mobile phase, use of nitrogen instead of helium as gas chromatography carrier gas, different stationary phase in HPLC, different concentrations in calibration solutions, use of another internal standard, etc.). 259

Requirements • As rule, -batch studies must be done using the CIPAC method for determination of the active ingredient content, if available. In case CIPAC method is (not) yet available, an in- • house method can be used. Nevertheless, the manufacturer has to apply as soon as possible with CIPAC for collaborative validation, and after possible adoption, bridging study is required as soon • as the CIPAC method becomes available. – The bridging study must be done as follows: minimum of same batches have to be analysed by the CIPAC method and in-house – method; Analysis at different days with independent sample weightings, independent calibrations. Raw data on all weightings, dilutions and details of the method must be provided. • he statistical evaluation by JMPS will include: • Comparison of RSD of CIPAC method and in-house method; Comparison of the value (repeatability) of the CIPAC method and in-house method. Based on that evaluation, bridging study can by accepted or rejected by JMPS. 260

APPENDIX EXAMPLES OF "IF REQUIRED" CLAUSES IN CERTAIN MODEL SPECIFICATIONS AND JUSTIFICATIONS FOR BEING ACTUALLY REQUIRED IN PARTICULAR PUBLISHED SPECIFICATIONS Clause Compound and Justification Specification Isomeric composition Cyfluthrin TC The ISO common name cyfluthrin does not provide definition of the composition of diastereomers in cyfluthrin technical material, therefore the any other relevant clause "if required" applies. Relevant impurities Malathion TC, DP, EC, Relevant impurities are identified in certain technical EW and UL materials only. There are relevant impurities identified in malathion TC and formulated products. Therefore, the clause of by-products of manufacture or storage "if required" applies. Description - Pirimiphos-methyl TC Pirimiphos-methyl technical material has limited stabilizer and formulated stability against hydrolysis and oxidation. stabilizer products is added to minimize degradation of the TC and formulated products in storage. Therefore, the information provided in the description clause for stabilizer “if required” applies. Acidity Pirimiphos-methyl TC As detailed above, excess acidity carried over from the manufacturing process may adversely affect the stability of the TC. Therefore, the acidity clause “if required” in the TC applies. Accelerated storage - Pirimiphos-methyl CS The relevant impurities may increase in accelerated relevant impurities, storage, as they are formed by degradation from the .. CS active ingredient. Therefore, sub-clause in the accelerated storage is required to ensure that the limits set under clause are still compliant. Suspensibility Thiamethoxam FS The suspensibility clause is not required as the FS is just little diluted before use and the application concentration exceeds the upper limit of CIPAC MT 184. (approx. 10 % /). Persistent foam Thiamethoxam FS The persistent foam clause is not required as the FS is just little diluted before use and the application concentration exceeds the upper limit of CIPAC MT 47.. Synergist content Deltamethrin + Piperonyl butoxide is included in the LN as piperonyl butoxide LN synergist. Therefore, the clauses of synergist content “if required” applies. 266

FAO In 2001, the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) and the World Health Organization (WHO) agreed to develop specifications for pesticides jointly, thus providing unique, robust and universally applicable standards for pesticide quality. This joint programme is based on Memorandum of Understanding between the two Organizations. This 2021 second edition of the Manual on development and use of FAO and WHO specifications for pesticides, which is only available online, supersedes the March 2020 third revision of the first edition and previous manuals and guidance documents published by either FAO or WHO on this subject. This Manual provides the standard process, unified requirements and procedures, harmonized definitions and nomenclature, technical guidelines and standards applicable to pesticides for use in agriculture and public health. FAO and WHO specifications for pesticides based on this manual are developed through the FAO/ WHO Joint Meeting on Pesticide Specifications (JMPS) and published on the web sites of the two Organizations. www.fao.org/pest-and-pesticide-management/guidelines-standards/faowho-joint- meeting-on-pesticide-specifications-jmps/en and https://extranet.who.int/pqweb/vector-control-products/who-specifications- pesticides FAO/WHO specifications apply only to the products of manufacturers whose technical materials have been evaluated. The specifications may be used to provide an international point of reference against which the quality of products can be judged, either for regulatory purposes or in commercial dealings, thereby helping to prevent the trade, sale and use of inferior quality pesticide products. Thus, the specifications will enhance confidence in the purchase and use of pesticides, and at the same time contribute towards better pest control, sound agricultural production, effective vector control measures and improved user, public and environmental safety throughout the world, especially in developing countries. Manual on the development and use of FAO and WHO specifications for chemical pesticides SECOND EDITION Manual on the development and use of FAO and WHO specifications for chemical pesticides
